intro:
The Wilderness of Sinai is the great desert of the Sinai peninsula and the surrounding wastes lying between Egypt and the land of Canaan — a triangle of granite, sand, and scattered oases through which Israel journeyed after the exodus. It embraces several named wildernesses (Shur, Sin, Sinai, Paran, and Zin) and is crowned by the mountain of God, Mount Sinai (Horeb). Here, for forty years, the redeemed nation was led, tested, fed, and formed into the people of the LORD.

Above all the wilderness was the school of the covenant. At Mount Sinai the LORD descended in fire and gave the Ten Words and the law, binding Israel to himself as a treasured possession and a kingdom of priests (Exodus 19:5–6; Exodus 20:1–2), and there the tabernacle was raised that God might dwell among them (Exodus 40:34). Yet it was also the place of Israel’s faithlessness — the golden calf (Exodus 32:4), the craving that brought the quail and plague at Kibroth-hattaavah (Numbers 11:33–34), and the unbelief at Kadesh that condemned that generation to die in the desert (Numbers 14:22–23).

Through all their wandering the LORD sustained them: manna from heaven each morning (Exodus 16:14–15), water from the rock at Rephidim (Exodus 17:6), the pillar of cloud and fire to lead them (Exodus 13:21), and the bronze serpent lifted up to heal the snake-bitten (Numbers 21:8–9). Their clothes did not wear out, and the LORD carried them “as a man carries his son” (Deuteronomy 1:31; Deuteronomy 8:4).

The New Testament reads the wilderness as both warning and shadow. Paul says these things “took place as examples” lest we fall as they did (1 Corinthians 10:11), for the rock that followed them was Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4). Jesus is the true manna, the bread of life come down from heaven (John 6:48–51), and the serpent lifted up foreshadows the Son of Man lifted on the cross for all who look and live (John 3:14–15). Led himself into the wilderness forty days, Jesus overcame where Israel failed — the faithful Son who brings his people through the desert into rest.

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Mount Sinai
The mountain of God where the law and tabernacle were given (Ex 19:20)
Rephidim
Where water flowed from the rock and Amalek was fought (Ex 17:6)
Marah
Bitter waters made sweet by the LORD (Ex 15:23–25)
Elim
Oasis of twelve springs and seventy palms (Ex 15:27)
Meribah
Place of quarreling over water; Moses struck the rock (Num 20:13)
Kibroth-hattaavah
‘Graves of craving,’ where the quail-plague struck (Num 11:34)
Hazeroth
Camp where Miriam and Aaron spoke against Moses (Num 12:1)
Wilderness of Paran
Vast desert from which the twelve spies were sent (Num 13:3)
Red Sea
Through whose waters Israel passed into the wilderness (Ex 15:22)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places and the events below appear on the map above.

cells: The Golden Calf
Israel’s idolatry at the foot of Sinai while Moses was on the mount (Ex 32:4)
The Quail and the Plague
Craving for meat answered in judgment at Kibroth-hattaavah (Num 11:31–33)
The Bronze Serpent
Lifted up to heal the bitten — a sign of Christ on the cross (Num 21:8–9)
Moses
Led Israel through the wilderness and received the law (Ex 19:3)
Aaron
High priest who also made the golden calf (Ex 32:2–4)
Miriam
Prophetess who led the song at the sea; rebuked at Hazeroth (Ex 15:20)
Joshua
Faithful spy and Moses’ attendant on the mountain (Num 14:6–9)
Caleb
Spy who, with Joshua, trusted the LORD to give the land (Num 13:30)
Korah
Led a rebellion against Moses and was swallowed by the earth (Num 16:32)
Jethro
Moses’ father-in-law who counseled him at the mountain (Ex 18:13–24)
